Strife is the condition of ongoing quarrel, conflict, and contention between people — the state where relationship has broken down into active hostility or persistent friction. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the immediate quarrel and the underlying provocation that keeps conflict alive. Strife is treated as a social pathology: it destroys community, poisons relationships, and reflects the disordered inner states of those who generate it. The peaceable person is not one who never disagrees but one who does not stir up or maintain strife.

Stubbornness is the refusal to be moved — the persistence of a chosen position or direction in the face of contrary evidence, wise counsel, or God's own appeal to change. The Hebrew vocabulary here captures the hardened, stiff quality: the stiff neck is the image of the person who refuses to turn. Stubbornness is different from faithful endurance: endurance holds to what is right under pressure, while stubbornness holds to what is wrong under appeal. Israel's stubbornness toward God is one of the central themes of the prophetic literature.

Stupor is a deprecated entry in this registry — the data originally filed under this word was correctly a study of sorrow, and has been fully transferred to that registry (#151). The word stupor itself, as the numbness and dullness of inner perception produced by overwhelming experience or judgment, is addressed within the broader vocabulary of spiritual blindness and hardness of heart. [Deprecated — data transferred to sorrow (#151).]

Submission is the voluntary placing of oneself under the authority of another — not enforced compliance but the willing acknowledgement of a proper ordering of relationship. The Greek vocabulary here is precise: to arrange oneself under, the military and relational sense of taking one's proper place in a structure of authority. Submission in Scripture is always contextual: it flows from love and wisdom rather than from domination. The supreme model is Christ's submission to the Father, which is the ground and example of all other submission.

Surrender is the giving up of self-governance — the inner act of letting go of one's claim to control one's own life, resources, or outcome. The Hebrew vocabulary here is built around the root for closing, shutting, and the coming out that happens when a city falls to a siege: surrender is what happens when resistance ends. In Scripture, surrender to God is not defeat but the beginning of genuine life: the self that is surrendered to God is not destroyed but restored. The theology of surrender runs through the entire biblical account of what it means to trust.

Temptation is the enticement toward what is wrong — the pull toward an action, attitude, or choice that would harm oneself, others, or one's relationship with God. The Greek vocabulary covers both the tempting and the testing: the same word is used for the trial that strengthens and the enticement that seeks to lead astray. Jesus is tempted in every way that humans are, yet without sin. Temptation is not sin: it is the moment before the choice. Scripture treats it as an unavoidable feature of creaturely life and promises a way through it.

Terror is the most extreme form of fear — the inner state produced by a threat so overwhelming that it shatters the capacity for rational response. The Hebrew vocabulary here is exceptionally rich: terror, trembling, dismay, shuddering, and the particular phrase terror on every side that appears in Jeremiah. Terror has a theological dimension: the Day of the Lord is described in terror vocabulary, and the enemies of God's people are struck with terror before them. It is the appropriate response of the creature to unconstrained power.

Testimony is the giving of witness — the public, verbal declaration of what one has seen, experienced, or knows to be true. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the formal witness in a legal setting and the personal declaration of what God has done. In the New Testament, testimony is one of the primary modes of gospel proclamation: the witness tells what they have seen and heard. Testimony is personal: it is the one who was there saying this is what happened. It connects inner experience to public truth.

Thought is the interior movement of the mind across ideas, memories, plans, and perceptions — the inner speech of the person reflecting on their world. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here is among the broadest in the corpus: saying to oneself, devising, planning, resembling, governing, reckoning. Thought in Scripture is morally freighted: the thought life is where intentions form, where desires take shape, where the fundamental orientation of the person is decided. The renewal of the mind is the renewal of thought.

Transformation is the process of genuine inner change — not superficial adjustment or improved performance but the actual remaking of what a person is. The Greek vocabulary covers the metamorphosis, the change from one form to another; the Hebrew covers the crossing over from one condition to another. In the New Testament, transformation is the work of the Spirit conforming the believer to the image of Christ: a process that begins in this life and is completed in the resurrection. [Replaced by transformation (#202).]

Transgression is the crossing of a boundary — specifically a boundary that has been clearly marked by God or legitimate authority, which the transgressor knew was there and chose to cross. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the act of crossing and the condition of having crossed: guilt, trespass, lawlessness. Transgression is wilful in a way that sin (which can be unintentional) is not: to transgress is to know the line and step over it. It is the vocabulary of deliberate moral violation.

Trust is the reliance upon the reliability of another — the inner act of leaning on someone else's strength, faithfulness, or goodness rather than on one's own resources. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers resting upon, taking refuge, the confident security of the person who has found someone worthy to lean on. Trust is the inner side of faith: where faith is the orientation of the whole person, trust is the specific inner act of resting on what one has faith in. The entire psalmic tradition is shaped around the movement between the collapse and the renewal of trust.

Truthfulness is the commitment to correspondence between what one says and what is actually the case — the quality of the person whose word is reliable and whose account of things can be trusted. The Greek vocabulary here (alētheia) covers truth in the deepest sense: not just accurate information but the genuine, unveiled reality of things. Truthfulness is both a character quality (the truthful person) and a relational practice (speaking truth to one another). It is grounded in the character of God, who cannot lie.

Unbelief is the refusal to receive and act on what God has said — not merely intellectual doubt but the settled unwillingness to trust. The Greek vocabulary covers both disobedience and unbelief as a single cluster, which is theologically significant: to disbelieve is to disobey, and to disobey is to disbelieve. Unbelief is not presented in Scripture primarily as an intellectual failure (though it may involve one) but as a relational rejection: the person who does not believe God has not received what he has offered.

Understanding is the capacity to grasp what something means — to perceive not just the surface of a thing but its significance, its inner logic, its connection to everything else. The Hebrew vocabulary (binah, sakal) covers both the insight that perceives and the intelligent action that results from perceiving rightly. Understanding in Scripture is never merely cerebral: it leads to action, to the fear of the Lord, to the reshaping of how one lives. Spiritual understanding is a gift, but it is also cultivated through attentiveness to what God has revealed.

Unity is the condition of many becoming genuinely one — not mere proximity or agreement but the actual shared life of those who are bound together in something deeper than circumstance. The Greek vocabulary here covers the oneness of those who share the same Spirit and the harmony of those who are genuinely aligned. The New Testament is particularly insistent on unity within the community of faith: the prayer of Jesus in John 17 is that his people would be one as he and the Father are one. Unity is both a gift and a calling.

Uprightness is the quality of the person who is straight — morally and relationally aligned with what is true and right, without the crooked turns of duplicity or self-interest. The Hebrew vocabulary covers the plain, the level, the path that does not deviate. Uprightness is related to integrity and righteousness but has a particular flavour: it is the quality of the person who goes straight, who does not take the roundabout path of manipulation or self-deception. The upright person is one in whose inner and outer life there is no hidden bend.

Vulnerability is the condition of being genuinely open — capable of being hurt, of being known at depth, of being affected by what happens. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here covers nakedness and uncovering: the state of being without the protective layers one normally carries. Scripture presents vulnerability as the necessary condition for genuine relationship: the person who cannot be wounded cannot really love or be loved. The exposure of the cross is the supreme image of divine vulnerability — God making himself open to rejection and suffering.

Weakness is the condition of being without sufficient strength to accomplish what is needed — the experience of one's own limitation and insufficiency. The Greek vocabulary here (astheneia) covers physical, moral, and spiritual weakness. In the New Testament, weakness is reframed rather than merely lamented: Paul's discovery is that God's power is most clearly visible in human weakness, that the strength that matters is not one's own. Weakness in this frame is not a problem to be solved but a condition to be inhabited honestly, which creates the space for divine strength.

Whoredom is the biblical vocabulary for unfaithfulness in the covenant — specifically the turning of devotion and service away from God toward other gods or other sources of security. The Hebrew root (zanah, fornication) is used metaphorically throughout the prophets to describe Israel's idolatry: the nation that was betrothed to God has pursued other lovers. The metaphor is chosen deliberately to capture the relational betrayal involved in idolatry — it is not merely rule-breaking but the breaking of a bond of love and fidelity.

Wickedness is the comprehensive term for the condition and conduct of the person who has fundamentally departed from what is right — living by what is harmful, unjust, and ungodly. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary spans harmful, unjust, and morally corrupt. Wickedness in Scripture is not merely bad behaviour but a settled orientation of the inner person away from God: the wicked person thinks and wants and chooses wrongly as a matter of character rather than accident. It is the spiritual condition that sin, left unaddressed, produces.

Will is the inner capacity to choose and act — the faculty by which a person decides what they want, what they will do, and whom they will serve. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here is the broadest in the entire corpus: heart, purpose, pleasure, deliberate action, the deep inclination of the person. Will in Scripture is neither the absolute self-determination of modern individualism nor the mere helplessness of determinism; it is the genuine but dependent freedom of the creature whose choices are real and whose character shapes those choices.

Wisdom is the capacity to live rightly in the world — to see what is true, choose what is good, and act in a way that fits with how things actually are. The Hebrew vocabulary (chokhmah, binah, sakal) covers both the practical skill of living and the deeper understanding that comes from knowing God. Wisdom begins with the fear of the Lord: the properly ordered relationship with God that makes genuine perception and right action possible. God himself is described as wise; human wisdom is a participation in and reflection of his.

Wonder is the inner response of the person who has encountered something that exceeds their capacity to contain or explain — the experience of being undone and enlarged at the same time. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers amazement, astonishment, the particular quality of being arrested and struck by what is greater than expected. Wonder in Scripture is not merely a pleasant aesthetic experience but a theological posture: it is the appropriate response to the works and ways of God, whose greatness and goodness continually exceed what could be anticipated.

Worship is the total orientation of the person toward God — expressed in posture, voice, action, and the ordering of the whole life around his worth. The Hebrew vocabulary covers bowing down (shachah) and the service or ministry (abad) that gives God what he is owed; the Greek adds the sense of doing homage. Worship is not merely what happens in a gathered service but the fundamental posture of the creature rightly relating to the Creator: giving God what he is worth, which is everything. It is the outer expression of the inner reality of love, trust, and reverence.

Worth is the intrinsic value of something — what it is actually worth, not what the market assigns to it or what flattery claims for it. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers both the likeness or comparison that establishes value and the formal valuation that sets a price. In Scripture, worth is ultimately theological: the worth of a human being derives from being made in the image of God and known by name. Worth is also relational: to be worthy of something is to be fittingly matched to it.

Wrath is the settled, purposeful anger of God against everything that corrupts, destroys, and opposes his good creation — not a loss of temper but the moral seriousness of one who will not let evil go unaddressed forever. The Hebrew vocabulary here is spare but intense. Wrath in Scripture is not God's most fundamental attitude toward humanity (love is) but it is his uncompromising response to sin. The gospel addresses wrath directly: what is owed is met, what threatened is turned away, and the wrath that would have fallen is received by the Son.

Yearning is the deep, physical ache of longing for someone loved and absent — the Hebrew root here (racham, compassion) connects yearning to the tender, womb-like love that longs to hold and protect what it loves. It is one of the most intimate words in the emotional vocabulary: not just wanting but aching, the longing of someone who loves deeply for the one who is away. It appears in only one verse in this registry, but the depth of the word is evident: this is love made urgent by absence.

Yielding is the act of giving way — producing what is asked, bearing what is expected, surrendering what one has been holding. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers sowing and bearing: the agrarian image of the ground that yields its fruit, and the person who delivers or leads or pays what is owed. Yielding is closely related to surrender and submission but is more concrete: it is the actual handing over, the productive giving of what was asked, rather than the inner orientation that precedes it.

Zeal is the burning intensity of concern for something one loves — the passionate energy that will not be neutral or half-hearted in relation to what one values. The Greek vocabulary here (spoudē, diligence) captures the eager, earnest quality of zeal rather than its most extreme form. In Scripture, zeal for God is consistently commended, but it must be aligned with knowledge: Paul's warning is that zeal without right understanding is destructive (Romans 10:2). Zeal for the wrong things, or for the right things by wrong means, is one of the great dangers of the religious person.

Soul (nephesh / psychē) is the most important single word in the entire corpus — the word that comes closest to meaning the living human person as a whole. The Hebrew nephesh originally meant throat or breath, then became the word for the appetite that drives a living creature, then expanded to mean the person's very life and self. The Greek psychē carries similar weight. When Jesus says whoever loses their life will find it, he uses this word. The soul is not a separate part of a person that can be detached; it is the whole person considered as a living, breathing, wanting being.

Heart (lev / kardia) is the most frequently referenced seat of the inner life in the entire biblical corpus — the Hebrew lev and its Greek equivalent kardia carrying a meaning far wider than our word heart. It is the centre of thought, decision, desire, will, emotion, and moral orientation: to think in one's heart, to set one's heart on something, to have a hard heart or a tender heart, to speak from the heart. The heart is the inner person in their totality, which is why the command to love God with all your heart is the command that encompasses everything else.

Spirit (ruach / pneuma) is the word that bridges the human and the divine — the Hebrew ruach meaning wind, breath, and spirit simultaneously. In the human person, spirit is the dimension of life that is turned toward God and capable of genuine relationship with him. In God, ruach is the creative, life-giving breath that animates creation and moves in history. In the New Testament, the Spirit is personalised as the Holy Spirit: not merely the spiritual dimension of life but God himself as the one who indwells and transforms. Spirit marks the point where the inner life opens to the transcendent.

Flesh (basar / sarx) is the word for the human person considered as embodied, creaturely, and mortal — made of matter, subject to hunger and pleasure and death. The Hebrew basar covers the physical body and, by extension, shared creatureliness: all flesh is mortal. The Greek sarx in Paul carries an additional weight: flesh becomes the term for the whole person considered as operating apart from the Spirit — the person whose inner life is governed by creaturely appetites rather than by God. Flesh is not evil in itself but becomes disordered when it rules the person rather than being ordered under God.

Gladness is the lighter, more spontaneous face of joy — the quick, warm delight that arises in the moment rather than the deep settled gladness that comes from a rightly ordered life. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers the being glad, the celebrating together, the simple well-being of a person whose circumstances and relationships are good. Gladness appears alongside joy throughout the psalms and wisdom literature, and it is attributed to God: he is glad over his people. It is one of the emotional signatures of the life lived in right relationship with God and others.

Strength is the capacity to act effectively in the world — not just physical power but the full range of inner and outer resources by which a person accomplishes what they are called to do. The Hebrew vocabulary is the most term-rich in the entire corpus: arm, bone, hand, might, firmness, greatness, power. Strength is both given by God (he gives strength to the weary) and drawn from him (I can do all things through him who strengthens me). The strength that Scripture most values is not the kind that operates independently of God but the kind that knows where it comes from.

Weeping is the bodily expression of grief, sorrow, or deep emotional intensity — the involuntary overflow of inner pain through tears and the sounds of crying. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the act of weeping and the tears themselves. Weeping in Scripture is never treated as weakness or failure: Jesus wept at the tomb of Lazarus. The psalms are full of weeping; Lamentations is written in tears. The promise of the final restoration is that God will wipe away every tear from their eyes — which means the tears were real and are known by him.

Malice is the deliberate desire to harm — the inner disposition that actively wishes ill on another and is pleased when they suffer. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers what is harmful and what is contemptible. Malice is treated in the New Testament as one of the residues of the old life that must be put away: it is listed alongside slander and hypocrisy as things incompatible with a life renewed by God. Malice is distinguished from anger (which responds to wrong) by its pleasure in harm: it is not triggered by injury but is a settled hostile orientation toward others.

Contempt is the inner dismissal of another as beneath consideration — the attitude that treats someone as worthless, ridiculous, or unworthy of respect. The Hebrew vocabulary covers to lighten, to despise, to taunt, to reject as of no account. Contempt is one of the most socially toxic of inner states because it is incompatible with genuine relationship: the contemptuous person cannot love the one they despise. Jesus's warning in the Sermon on the Mount treats contempt (calling someone a fool) with the same gravity as murder, because both proceed from the same inner dismissal of the other's worth.

Doubt is the inner state of being pulled in two directions at once — unable to settle fully on trust or disbelief, confident and uncertain at the same moment. The Greek vocabulary here covers both the act of judging (weighing the evidence) and the genuine uncertainty that remains unsettled. Scripture distinguishes doubt from unbelief: Thomas doubts but is not condemned; the disciples doubt but are sent. Doubt in Scripture is often the companion of genuine faith rather than its opposite — the honest wrestling of a person who is trying to trust in the face of difficulty.

Comfort is the bringing of genuine relief to the person in pain — not the minimising of their suffering but the provision of strength, companionship, and hope that makes the suffering bearable. The Hebrew nacham and the Greek paraklēsis cover both the consoling and the encouraging: comfort is what the Comforter does, and the Comforter is the Spirit. God is the Father of all comfort. Comfort is always relational: it requires the presence of someone who sees the pain and responds to it with something that actually helps.

Craving is the most acute form of desire — the pressing, urgent want that will not wait and has difficulty being satisfied. The Greek vocabulary here covers both the sick longing of intense desire and the aspiration toward something felt as necessary. Craving is desire at the level of physical urgency: the body (or the person considered bodily) pressing toward what it needs or has been trained to want. Scripture is not contemptuous of craving in itself, but it consistently asks whether the craving is rightly directed.

Blessing is the positive flow of divine favour and goodness toward a person, community, or the world — and the human act of speaking that favour over others and back to God. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the bestowing of benefit (God blessing) and the spoken declaration that invokes or honours that benefit (humans blessing). Blessing has a social and physical concreteness in the Old Testament: to be blessed is to have children, land, prosperity, and peace. In the New Testament it is spiritualised without being removed from the concrete: blessed are those who are poor in spirit.

Spiritual powers is a placeholder category for the non-human spiritual beings described in Scripture — angels, demons, principalities, powers, and the various named beings that populate the biblical world. This category does not have its own Hebrew or Greek term but is a synthesis point for the programme: understanding the inner life of the human person requires understanding the spiritual environment within which that person exists, including the beings that can affect, inhabit, or oppose them. [Special handling — cross-registry synthesis placeholder.]

Power is the capacity to accomplish, to cause things to happen, to exercise effective influence in the world. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here is broad: authority, ability, kingdom, salvation, dominion. Power in Scripture is not morally neutral: it matters greatly who has it, what they do with it, and where it comes from. God is the ultimate source of all genuine power; human power is derived and delegated. The New Testament is particularly concerned with the power of the Spirit — the power that enables what would be impossible by ordinary human means.

Authority is the right to exercise power — the legitimacy that distinguishes command from mere force. The Greek vocabulary here covers ruling, lordship, and the right to act with binding effect. Jesus teaches as one who has authority, not as the scribes. In the New Testament, authority is both delegated (the apostles, the governing authorities) and absolute (God's authority, Christ's authority over all things). Authority corrupts when it is exercised for the benefit of the one who holds it rather than those under it.

Might is the most physically concentrated vocabulary for power in the entire corpus — the raw, muscular strength that accomplishes by force what cannot be accomplished by subtlety. The Hebrew vocabulary here is the largest term set of any registry, including horn (the image of concentrated animal power), arm, strength in its most energetic forms. God's might is the subject of some of the most exalted poetry in the Old Testament. Human might is real but derivative and limited: the mighty are humbled, but God's might is the ground of all that stands.

Dominion is the exercise of authority over a domain — the governance, ordering, and stewardship of what has been placed under one's charge. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers ruling, having dominion, lording over, and kingdom. The word appears in the creation narrative: humanity is given dominion over the earth, which is a mandate for care and governance rather than exploitation. The question in Scripture is always whether dominion is exercised as God exercises it — with justice, mercy, and care — or in the pattern of fallen lordship that uses others for one's own benefit.

Energy is the capacity for active work and effort — the inner drive and outer force that enables doing. The Greek vocabulary here (energeia) covers the working activity that produces results. In the New Testament it appears primarily in theological contexts: the energy at work in the believer is God's energy working in and through them (Colossians 1:29). Human energy is real but is sustained and directed by divine energy: it is the working out of what God works in. [XREF: covered by power (#196).]

Image (tselem / eikōn) is the term for the imprint or likeness of something — the representation that carries the form and character of what it represents. In creation, humans are made in the image of God: they are his representatives and counterparts in the world, bearing something of his character and acting on his behalf. In Christ, the image vocabulary is used at its highest: he is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15), the exact representation of the divine nature. The renewal of humanity is described as the restoration of the image.

Transformation is the process of genuine change from one form into another — not improvement but genuine metamorphosis, the inner person actually becoming different rather than merely behaving differently. The Greek vocabulary here (metamorphoō) is the same root as the transfiguration. In the New Testament, transformation is the work of the Spirit: as believers behold the glory of the Lord, they are transformed into the same image, from glory to glory (2 Corinthians 3:18). Transformation is both process and destination: it begins now and is completed in the resurrection.

Treachery is the betrayal of trust for personal advantage — specifically the hidden, deliberate decision to work against someone who believes they are safe with you. The Greek vocabulary here (prodotēs, traitor) covers the one who hands over what was entrusted to them. Judas's act is described with this vocabulary. Treachery is distinguished from ordinary conflict by its exploitation of trust: the traitor uses the relationship itself as the means of harm. It is one of the most theologically weighty forms of relational violation.

Name (shem / onoma) carries far more weight in the biblical world than a mere label: a name expresses the essence of what a person or being is, their character, their history, their destiny. To know someone's name is to have access to who they really are. God's name is holy — not merely his title but his very self. To call on the name of the Lord is to reach for the person behind the word. To give a name is to establish a relationship; to have one's name written in the book of life is to be known and held by God.

Resentment is the slow-burning anger that refuses to be released — the holding of grievance over time, nursing the memory of wrongs done and keeping the account open. It differs from righteous anger (which seeks resolution) and from grief (which mourns without hostility). The biblical vocabulary for this concept is embedded in the anger and bitterness families. Resentment is spiritually corrosive: it poisons the person who holds it, keeps old wounds open, and prevents the renewal that forgiveness and reconciliation make possible.

Vulnerability is the condition of being genuinely open and exposed — capable of being wounded, affected, or known at depth. The Hebrew vocabulary here is built on uncovering and nakedness: the state of the person who has no protective shell. In Scripture, vulnerability is the necessary condition for genuine love and genuine relationship: the person who cannot be hurt cannot really give themselves. The cross is the supreme image of divine vulnerability — God in Christ making himself open to rejection, suffering, and death. It is the form love takes when it is fully serious.

Blindness (spiritual) is the condition of the inner person whose capacity to perceive spiritual and moral reality has been impaired or closed — not the failure of physical sight but the failure of the deeper seeing by which one recognises God, truth, and what is real. The Greek vocabulary covers both darkening and the condition of blindness. Jesus heals physical blindness as a sign of the deeper healing he brings: he is the light of the world, and those who follow him will not walk in darkness. Spiritual blindness can be the result of judgment, of choice, or of deception.

Sloth is the failure to invest the effort that responsibility and love require — the habitual preference for rest over work, for comfort over the demands of one's calling. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here covers sluggishness and the quality of going slack. Sloth is not mere tiredness but the settled orientation of the person who will not press through the friction of genuine engagement. It is treated in Scripture as a moral failure: the servant who buries his talent is the servant who preferred safety to the risk of genuine investment.

Likeness (homoiōsis) is the Greek word that captures the quality of resemblance — being like, having the same form as. It appears alongside image in Genesis (made in our image and likeness) and in the New Testament in Paul's description of the transformation of believers into the likeness of Christ. Likeness is the relational and experiential dimension of the image: where image points to the formal representation, likeness points to the actual similarity that results from genuine relationship. To become like Christ is the goal of the transformation process.

Deadness is the condition of what once lived being no longer alive — the absence of responsiveness, vitality, and capacity for relationship. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both physical death and the spiritual condition of being dead while alive. Paul uses deadness to describe humanity's condition apart from God: dead in trespasses and sins. The resurrection of Jesus is the reversal of deadness: not merely the resuscitation of what was dying but the restoration of genuine life, more fully alive than before.

Being — the bare fact of existing, of having reality — is the deepest and most fundamental category of all, underlying everything else in the corpus. The biblical vocabulary for this distributes itself across soul (nephesh, the living being), spirit (ruach, the animated being), and the basic ontological affirmations of creation: God is, God causes to be, the creature is in relation to the Creator. To be is not taken for granted in Scripture: existence is given, sustained, and known by God. [XREF: covered by soul (#182).]
